speaker: interviewer
text: Leon Wells, interview tape seven. Dr. Wells--

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon Wells, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were telling me about the arrest of Rauch and your friend, and they got in touch with you. Would you just tell us Mr. Rauch's position in the camp at Janowska?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the deputy-- not Janowska, the Sonderkommando 1005. He was a deputy. He was a young man. And scharfuhrer he was. And he was arrested, but they ran to me. And I-- the American-- I don't know, it was headquarters or something like this-- either way, called me up to say what to investigate if I feel like he's the man that they should, as an ex-Nazi, to deliver him to Poland or not, I don't know. So I came down, recognized him, made my statement.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he recognize you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He recognized me. He knew me because we worked for the Death Brigade, the Sonderkommando for five months, four and 1/2 months, five months and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he didn't deny?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did not deny. He did not get to anything, but, but, but-- you know what the reason is. And the American lawyer or it's American officer, whatever he was at the main headquarters, took down everything that I was talking. And afterwards he said to me, I'm going out. I have to go to do something, but I will be back in about 10 minutes, or five minutes, or 20 minutes. And he has a stick, he has this, but don't do anything near the window, or if you do something that you kill him or something while I'm not here, you will be tried for killing the man. You cannot do this.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he gave you a stick?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, [NON-ENGLISH]. But he said, he did not give me anything. But if I do something against-- if I kill him, I will be tried for murder. And he walked out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had this problem, what to do in these 10 or 20 minutes? I don't think I remember. Because if I don't do anything it means-- I had my philosophy and so on, and he was not exactly what he says himself or told me, and if I do something, I can be-- somebody comes and says, he beat up a man or he killed a man. If I kill him, I am really in trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you have to do something. And it's not my feeling it's brought to trial and it's finished. So forcing myself, I had to hit him a few times, and that was all. He was arrested, and he was sent to Poland for trial. While he was sent to Poland, his mother and sister came to me. They knew where I live and started talk to me. You know, after all, it's my son, that's my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And how about-- what if we can do anything for you, we will do, but what can we do about your parents or your family? We cannot bring them back to life or if we can do. And he was a young man, a kid, and so on, and whatever. They talked to me, but I felt-- I'd never offended them or did anything, but I felt-- I said, it's not my business. It's Polish law at the Polish court, and what they will.

speaker: interviewee
text: In meantime, in Poland was still a man by the name Roth-- R-O-T-H-- whose son was killed in the Death Brigade, was with me, and I write about him right in the beginning. Because he was a young man, my age, and he was killed. And the father, we visited him later in Israel-- he moved to Israel from Poland-- was from a very rich home. He was the representative of, I believe, Texaco, one of the big oil companies of Poland. And he was well-to-do. And he even got-- you say, wow that he even got the company sends me a monthly package from UNRRA from America. We used to get from America. And he followed there, and he took over the trial there. And from him I found out he was hanged afterwards in Poland. But otherwise, I wouldn't know anything to what happened. He was delivered to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in 1947?

speaker: interviewee
text: '47, '46-- '47, the beginning of '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at this point, you were a student.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a student at Technische Hochschule. It's a technical university of Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Munich. And what were the circumstances under which you were able to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: The circumstances I was-- I got quite a few scholarships, one form Portugal, one from Switzerland, and one from America-- Lehigh University in America. And I choose Lehigh University. And I went as a student to Lehigh University. In meantime-- let us be a little bit more truthful about it-- I meantime, the Jewish Labor Committee, the Arbeter Ring, in New York, send me also affidavit, which I did not need or did not use or anything. I was-- I met with them one time to thank them, and that was all that I had to do with them. And on, I never knew the Labor Committee. They never knew my family, but somebody here said name, make that happen, and they send me too. So two situations that I could have come to America, but under--

speaker: interviewer
text: You chose Lehigh University.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I choice first Lehigh because I felt for a year I can learn a little bit English. I did not speak any English, anything. And meet the people, I did not know where-- from the first day, I could only go to Mrs. Friedman's that they were in America, but they were-- he was my teacher, but I did not know even where to go, what to do the next day in America. To get something, I did not have any relatives any place in this world.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came here and went to Lehigh?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Lehigh for a year, and after Lehigh I went and worked at NYU in the graduate school for mathematics and mechanics-- the Courant is it's called.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get you PhD?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got it in '49. Before I left, I got my PhD, but I came here and mechanical engineering, can I change it to here to study physics? And afterwards I went-- at NYU I was research associate of mathematics, applied mathematics. You're-- if I had my family, anybody of profession, of anything, you'll bumble around to whatever. You can and you grab, you do. I did not have a direction.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, then, a little bit about how you met your wife and the birth of your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: How I met my wife was through a friend. I was at Chess Club, Manhattan Chess Club in the Village, and I played there chess. And there was a young lady that worked together in the laboratory in a hospital in Brooklyn, in New York. And she had a party, and she introduced me to my wife. And this how we met. And got married. And we have three children, nice children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names? And your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is Frieda, and my children's name is Beth Ann, Alan Hilary, and Julian.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you begin to become involved in the additional War Crimes Trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not begin, but because of these in Poland, having had the first book published, everybody knew me. So when I came to Germany, the first thing was, where can I get something, a job or to work? The Historical Commission, that was with the Jewish committee. So now I am here. Now and with this book published, when they had the-- now one moment more. There was a Russian by the name Belyaev. He was very high of, I believe, one of the 12 committees commit man, of the Communist party in Moscow. He was also a journalist, and he wrote in Lemberg, the story of Lwow, Lwow, Lemberg, or whatever you call it. And he also, because of my manuscript and so on, the first thing that he got in touch was with me. And we became very friendly, and he was in the Communist party. And he had with me one day-- he told me one day, Leon-- he called me [NON-ENGLISH], Russian for Leon. You know, you went through a lot. I am a Communist, and I believe in Communism, and I will die, but it will take us another hundred years to get someplace. You went through a lot. Try to move to the West. And if you need any help, I will give it to you. I will write to the border police to let you through to Poland, and from Poland you keep moving. So you had these too, and Belyaev, he would say. And so Belyaev wrote also a lot for, books for, and also they organized for the Nuremberg Trials. So again, my name came in. And from Poland, from the Historical Commission, my name came in there.

words.text: and so on, the first thing

speaker: interviewer
text: So before you know it, you are reference, reference. And every reference, even if it starts from nothing, becomes a reference. And so I went. And so in Germany, where any time they had the trials of the people from East Galicia or from Poland, I was called as a witness. I was called.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I had this double problem. On the one hand, I want to make-- finish with it. The other hand, I also felt a duty of being a witness, and how can you say as a witness, no, you don't want to be a witness. You don't want to say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have these double problems, and before you know it, you are more and more involved in it. And if one calls you, the other keeps calling you. It's reference, a reference. So in 1959, I became a witness in the Eichmann Trial. I was in the Nuremberg Trials. They get me in the Eichmann Trial. Who were you a witness against in Nuremberg, in the Nuremberg Trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in general about Poland. It wasn't any particular. And so, after the Eichmann Trial, I had-- we lived in Paris for this time for three, four months. And so the people started to call me and also a publisher called me. So I published there a book [NON-ENGLISH]. This book became therefore a big seller, not a best seller, but the big seller, a few editions. And so it went, and it was translated into 12 languages and 14, 15 different countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: I meantime, in East Germany, they published a book-- and what's its name? The very known writer, Jewish-German writer. He became the president of the writers union. And he wrote the forward, and before you know it he paralleled to me, and so on. And so you are becoming more and more as a part of this trials. And one trial leads to another, and every time, they need a witness, they called on me. What were your impressions about the participants in the trials and the proceedings?

speaker: interviewee
text: My impressions were very, very weak. It is so you came, you told your story, and got out. You did not even look because I wasn't there, for any trial, the Eichmann Trial or any trial, to become part of, you did, I did, now let's shake hands, or not shake hands, or let us call it now equal. You see, you killed my parents, I killed you, now we are equal, or any place.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in any place, I did not have any interest to look at them. I told the story as it is. I told about his name, and like, for example, even when-- in one place was even a little mistake in a name, and I said, I knew what his name, nobody introduced, and nobody-- and I never tried even to do it more or less do it, but I felt if I'm called, and very often, like for example, in Saarbrucken, when I-- but I was sick at this time. And they said they will supply me with a nurse. A nurse-- my wife came, and a nurse was sitting next to me, give me pills. But how can you say no? You don't want to go. You don't want to do it. Who was on trial in Saarbrucken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in Saarbrucken, Gebauer, Fritz Gebauer. And you have it so-- I don't know what to say and so on, but it's a little bit more than-- you see, the world has-- we all speak about a balance sheet. I don't have any balance sheets. I don't want any balance sheets. I don't think about any balance sheets.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what do you think for the world of this legacy of having had the Nuremberg trials, the Eichmann trials, and the punishment related to that? What do you think the legacy is for society and for the world? What do you-- do you think that these trials impacted the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it will impact if people that will say maybe there will be-- I believe that when we did not do anything about Armenians in Turkey, we did the first mistake because afterwards was a joke. You see, this what happens. Maybe the different way is different, and maybe not, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your newest book,

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about and about your religious faith after having survived the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is what the title say-- shattered faith. I was very faithful. I became disso-- not only disillusioned, but I feel that suffering in all of these and punishment is not a way of anybody, including people to punish other people. I am not for death sentence. I am not for certain things that a lot of people feel differently.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I feel so what? It does not do anything. But a shattered faith is for very simple being disillusioned, or disappointed, or seeing that exactly the fate-- let me say it this way. When I stood in 1942 digging my own grave-- and I did not think about anything. As I said, I had high temperature and was sick-- the worst sickness-- but the one thing I remember afterwards-- maybe afterwards, maybe at this time-- I won't think for sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see I am-- they want to kill me because I am different. And this time I had the whole family. I was the only one to be killed because I am-- we are different. And if somebody says to me, but we don't want to take the difference. We don't want to do it different. We want to be kill, be killed. And that's not my-- and the shows it. And there's yesterday-- it was funny. Yesterday I got this review from the book of this few professors and me. And afterwards, they said those about I am talking also with Spielberg Foundation. And I said to them, couldn't be the same Spielberg, because only a week or two weeks ago I spoke to them. They couldn't have-- at least that they publish and send from Kentucky. My press agent said to me that they come. I said, at the end of his life, he's now writing about this-- the philosophy of his life. And that is, I started with the Death Brigade, I was 21-- 20, or 19, or 18-- and now it is my philosophy of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were raised as a very orthodox, Hasidic man, in a very orthodox family. What is your religious observance now? And how has the war changed--

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't worn-- the war changed-- I don't believe in anything. I don't believe in a God. I keep certain things because of-- the same as I keep Thanksgiving. Not because I believe in them or not believe in them. It's American, oh, fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am a part of it. My children are a part of it. And you have also to live with your children, not only with you. Are you a beggar? But to speak about it, the religion has any today that I would recommend. I would-- I'm keeping out of it. It's not my business. Is there anything that you would like to add this time? Anything that I didn't ask you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thank you very much for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You wanted to call my wife?

speaker: interviewer
text: We will. We'll do that. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are two pages of my manuscript, which I have the whole one, about the Death Brigade, written during the war, I believed then in the Death Brigade before and afterwards continued in the hiding place in 1943, '44. This picture is from my book, which appeared at the beginning of 1946, but this picture was taken in '45, preparing for the production of this book by the Jewish Historical Commission in Lodz, Poland. This picture is a picture of my family-- my three sons-- one, two, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you describe the top row, from left to right, give the names.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, on the left side is Alan Wells. And one, two, three-- the next man, the second from the right, is Julian, my youngest. And sitting, the first woman on the right side is my daughter Beth Ann Wells. And afterwards, it's me holding the youngest grandchild, which I have now a younger one, and the left side my wife, Frieda Wells.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Wells, we'd like you to introduce us to your bride.

speaker: interviewee
text: My bride-- That's my wife, Frieda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you tell us, briefly, how Leon's war experiences have affected your lives-- your life, his life vis-a-vis your family in raising your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would say that it's an ever present factor, but it isn't a disturbing factor in that it decimated the family. The children grew up really rather healthy and normal. And when there is talk about second generation, they are aware of it, but nobody feels at this point now that they're all grown ups, that they were shattered by this knowledge and crippled by it. So they grew up rather normal, healthy, more involved in what had happened during the war than other children would have been, and more sympathetic. And I think there is a real pride in their father of having survived, not physically only-- because they wouldn't be here if he hadn't-- but that he is a humanist and that he has a positive, more-than-average feeling for humanity. And that he hasn't become bitter. And they all read his book as they became old enough when they themselves wanted to read it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was that? And how difficult was it to explain to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was somehow normal. It even goes on now to my daughter's children, who like to hear about the past, and they are seven and eight and 1/2 now. And they have known for a while that their grandfather was in the war. They don't know anything about being victimized, about the Jewish aspect of it, but they know he is a war survivor, and that none of the family is around. And I suppose in the same way, right, it went with our children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yet, but I feel in spite what they are saying, being myself-- being our self, now grandparent, that my children did not have grandparents, did not have uncles, aunts, or grandparents, or anybody. You know, we were by ourselves completely. And I mean by completely, like even everyday life ourselves. So how much does the fact--

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents died young, so there really was not an extended family at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: We thank you very much for sharing with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait. We're still not finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh boy.
